items.title: The UK Is Planning a Social Media Curfew for 16- and 17-Year-Olds
items.summary_raw: The restrictions, which can be turned off, will include a crackdown on “addictive” app features and will be in addition to a total ban on children under 16 accessing platforms like TikTok and YouTube.

items.summary_raw: New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed an order pausing construction of large data centers — the first statewide pause in the country.

items.title: Spain sparkles to shock France 2-0 and advance to the World Cup final
items.summary_raw: Spain is back in the men's World Cup final for the first time since 2010. That's also when Spain won its only World Cup title. France had hoped to reach the final for the third straight tournament.

items.title: Watch: The clash between US and Iran for control of the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary_raw: The BBC's Barbara Plett Usher examines the latest tensions over the key international shipping channel.

items.title: Eating chili peppers may raise the risk of one deadly cancer
items.summary_raw: A major review found that people who consumed the most chili peppers had a substantially higher risk of esophageal cancer, though the evidence was less clear for stomach and colorectal cancers. Researchers emphasize that the findings show an association, not proof of cause and effect, and that more research is needed to determine whether moderate consumption carries similar risks.

items.title: Counties face $52M in food stamp costs after NC budget omits funding request
items.summary_raw: Counties are absorbing higher administrative costs tied to the food stamp program as federal reimbursements drop. State lawmakers left additional funding out of their newly approved budget.

items.title: Drought conditions make hot weather even hotter
items.summary_raw: While the lack of rainfall is an obvious part of North Carolina's ongoing drought, there's another side effect that many people don't realize. Drought conditions can make hot weather even hotter.

items.title: YouTube and X Have Become ‘Gateways’ to Nudify Apps
items.summary_raw: A new study found that social media platforms are referring people to sites where they can create nonconsensual, sexually explicit deepfakes for as little as $1 an image.

items.title: In the aftermath of deadly shootings, ICE pauses most traffic stops
items.summary_raw: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement will pause non-urgent vehicle stops after two deadly shootings in less than a week, Maine U.S. Sen. Angus King's office tells NPR.

items.title: Questions continue about ICE shooting in Maine
items.summary_raw: Investigations are underway in Southern Maine, one day after a fatal shooting by an ICE agent. The agency says it was seeking a different person. Protests and vigils continue.

items.summary_raw: A man was nearly sucked head-first out of a cabin window mid-air on a Ryanair plane.

items.title: Are there aliens on exoplanet K2-18b? Scientists just scanned it for signals
items.summary_raw: K2-18b is one of the most promising worlds for the search for extraterrestrial life, so astronomers conducted an unusually powerful radio survey using both the VLA and MeerKAT telescopes. Advanced software analyzed millions of signals, filtering out Earth-based interference and other false positives. No convincing artificial radio transmissions were found, but the project demonstrated a powerful new approach that will make future SETI searches faster and far more effective.

items.title: Man gets life in prison for killing girlfriend, trying to kill her son in Johnston County
items.summary_raw: A man accused of killing his girlfriend and shooting her son inside a Johnston County home in March will spend the rest of his life in prison.

items.title: Lookup Thursday before sunrise for a SpaceX launch

items.title: New York Governor Signs First Statewide Data Center Moratorium
items.summary_raw: “We have no choice but to address the challenges created by these massive facilities,” New York governor Kathy Hochul said. The executive order will pause construction for one year.

items.title: A 37-year soil experiment revealed a hidden climate threat
items.summary_raw: The world's longest-running soil warming experiment has revealed an unexpected climate concern. After nearly four decades, researchers found that warming can cause microbes to break down stable soil carbon that scientists once believed was largely protected. That releases extra carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, potentially accelerating global warming.

items.title: Popular weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy may slow biological aging
items.summary_raw: Researchers found that semaglutide, the active ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy, slowed biological aging markers in adults with HIV, marking the first clinical evidence that the drug may influence human aging. Although the findings are encouraging, scientists say larger studies are needed before concluding that the medication can help people age more slowly.

The discovery suggests the giant carnivore delivered an incredibly powerful face-to-face bite, offering new clues about how Tyrannosaurus hunted.

items.title: NASA's Perseverance just completed a marathon on Mars
items.summary_raw: NASA's Perseverance rover has reached an impressive new milestone on Mars, completing the equivalent of a full marathon by driving 26.2 miles (42.195 kilometers) across the Red Planet. It accomplished the feat in just five years and four months, reaching the distance on its 1,890th Martian day, less than half the time it took the previous record holder, NASA's Opportunity rover.

items.title: NASA selects four new Moon missions to build a permanent lunar base
items.summary_raw: NASA is ramping up its lunar ambitions by awarding nearly $600 million for four commercial Moon landings planned for late 2028. Each mission will carry the same trio of science instruments to improve lunar navigation, study dangerous dust kicked up during landings, and map the Moon's radiation environment. The agency also revealed plans for new rovers, communication satellites, and additional cargo missions as it lays the groundwork for a permanent Moon Base.

Because humans show signs of having the same pathway, the research could pave the way for more effective treatments for conditions such as eczema.

items.title: Alan Turing's biggest AI assumption may have been wrong
items.summary_raw: A new book claims AI has been built on a flawed assumption dating back to Alan Turing's famous 1950 paper. Peter J. Denning argues that the most important parts of human intelligence, including common sense, intuition, culture, and practical know-how, cannot be encoded into computers. He believes this makes true human-level AI impossible, regardless of how large language models become.

items.title: New dark matter theory could solve multiple cosmic mysteries at once
items.summary_raw: Dark matter may be far more complicated than scientists once believed. A new study suggests it could consist of at least two different kinds of particles that slowly separate over time, with heavier particles sinking toward the centers of galaxies and lighter ones drifting outward. This simple idea could explain several puzzling cosmic observations that have frustrated astronomers for years, from unusually diffuse dwarf galaxies to surprisingly dense dark matter clumps that bend light through gravitational lensing.

items.title: Losing just 80 minutes of sleep a night could make you gain weight
items.summary_raw: Sleeping about an hour and 20 minutes less each night for six weeks caused participants to gain weight and spend more time inactive. Researchers found that even mild, realistic sleep loss, similar to what many adults experience, had measurable effects. They warn that if this pattern continues over months or years, the health consequences could become much more significant, including a higher risk of diabetes and heart disease.

items.title: A 200-year-old physics experiment could help build future computers

Instead of relying on expensive, highly engineered materials, they simply shine a laser at a tiny circular disc, producing stable swirling patterns in light that researchers believe could one day help power advanced data storage, communications, and computing technologies.

items.title: Watch: Horses evacuated as fire approaches riding centre near Paris
items.summary_raw: The Fontainebleau forest blaze, described by officials as of 'exceptional scale', continues to rage for the second day.

items.title: Scientists discover how the brain rewires itself to truly multitask
items.summary_raw: Practice may do more than make perfect. Researchers found that extensive training physically reorganizes the brain, allowing learned tasks to bypass the prefrontal cortex and run through specialized circuits instead. By freeing the brain's "thinking" center, people became better at performing another task at the same time, challenging the long-held idea that humans only switch rapidly between tasks rather than truly multitask.

items.title: Sam Neill Inspired a Generation of Scientists
items.summary_raw: The actor, who died at age 78 on Monday, embodied a passionate approach to research and positive masculinity in his role as paleontologist Alan Grant in the 1993 blockbuster Jurassic Park.

items.title: Volkswagen planning to cut up to 100,000 jobs globally
items.summary_raw: The group, which includes Porsche and Audi, has faced a steep fall in profits and tough competition from China.

items.title: Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction
items.summary_raw: Why do beaches today have seashells from clams and snails instead of brachiopods? A new study suggests the answer lies in Earth's greatest mass extinction, when warming oceans and falling oxygen levels wiped out animals that couldn't adapt. Species with body plans and metabolisms better suited to the changing conditions survived and went on to dominate the seas, offering a glimpse of how modern marine life could respond to climate change.

items.title: Siri AI Is Becoming Apple’s Everything Tool
items.summary_raw: Apple’s revamped Siri is more than a voice assistant; it’s now the backbone of the iPhone user experience. You can try it now through the iOS 27 public beta.

items.title: UK-Switzerland deal to scrap roaming charges and allow Britons to use e-gates
items.summary_raw: The UK signs a new services deal with Switzerland it says will increase exports by £5.2bn annually "in the long run".

items.title: Columbia scientists discover surprising link between serotonin and heart valve disease
items.summary_raw: Scientists have uncovered evidence that serotonin, the chemical best known for regulating mood, may also speed the progression of a common heart valve disease in some people. The research suggests that patients with degenerative mitral regurgitation who take SSRI antidepressants and carry a specific genetic variant may develop severe valve damage sooner, potentially requiring surgery at a younger age.

items.title: The Problem With VAR at the 2026 World Cup Isn’t the Technology—It’s Who Interprets It
items.summary_raw: The video assistant referee system, or VAR, has led to some controversial calls at the 2026 World Cup. Here’s why.

items.title: A versatile actor whose roles went far beyond Jurassic Park - Sam Neill dies aged 78
items.summary_raw: The actor built up a long string of screen credits over a five-decade career.

items.title: Why gold never tarnishes has finally been explained
items.summary_raw: Gold may have a secret self-defense system that helps it resist tarnishing. Researchers discovered that atoms on gold surfaces reorganize themselves into patterns that block oxygen from reacting with the metal, suppressing oxidation by up to a trillion-fold. Beyond explaining why gold jewelry stays bright for generations, the finding could help scientists create more powerful gold-based catalysts for manufacturing and clean energy.

items.title: Wi-Fi 8 Explained: Features, Release Date, and More
items.summary_raw: Chipset makers and router manufacturers are talking about Wi-Fi 8, but what is the new standard, and when will it arrive?

items.title: The SpaceX IPO made history. One month on has it lost momentum?
items.summary_raw: A month on from the firm's stock market debut, the reality of how SpaceX currently makes money has seemed to come into clearer focus.

items.title: Yale scientists may have found how Parkinson's disease spreads through the brain
items.summary_raw: Yale scientists discovered two neuron surface proteins that appear to help spread the toxic protein linked to Parkinson’s disease. Blocking these proteins in mice dramatically reduced disease progression, offering a potential new target for future therapies.

items.title: Tesla Says It’s Building a Wheelchair-Accessible Robotaxi
items.summary_raw: A Tesla representative at a DC hearing said the vehicle is “an active product being built.” But its timeline isn’t clear.

items.title: German court convicts Iraqi couple of enslaving Yazidi girls
items.summary_raw: The pair, named only as Twana H.S. and Asia R. A., left Germany for Iraq in 2015 and became members of the Islamic State group.

items.title: Common blood pressure drug could make cancer therapy far more powerful
items.summary_raw: Researchers found that the common blood pressure drug telmisartan can significantly improve the performance of the cancer drug olaparib, potentially expanding its benefits beyond patients with BRCA-related tumors. The combination is already being tested in human clinical trials after showing strong immune-boosting and anticancer effects in preclinical studies.

items.title: There Are Endless Conspiracy Theories About Lindsey Graham’s Death
items.summary_raw: Despite a complete lack of evidence, everyone from Russia to Israel and Iran is being blamed for Lindsey Graham’s death.

items.title: People flee Bangkok bar as huge flames shoot out from doorway
items.summary_raw: At least 28 people have died and 25 are critically injured after a fire tore through a bar in a popular Bangkok district.

items.title: Experimental drug reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
items.summary_raw: An experimental drug called DT-109 reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by repairing the gut and preventing harmful toxins from damaging the liver. The discovery could open the door to a new class of treatments for MASH and potentially other diseases tied to gut health.
